programs.name: Med-QUEST Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
programs.what_it_does: HCBS waivers under Med-QUEST provide community-based alternatives to nursing home care, including personal care, adult day health, and homemaker services. These enable seniors to receive tailored support at home rather than in institutions.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors or disabled individuals eligible for nursing facility level care but preferring home-based services, meeting Med-QUEST financial criteria.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by waiver; includes up to 80-120 hours/month personal care, respite, and equipment; prevents institutionalization.

programs.name: Hawai‘i PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Hawai‘i PACE offers comprehensive medical and social services to frail seniors, including primary care, hospitalization, prescription drugs, and adult day health. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team.
programs.who_its_for: Community-dwelling adults 55+ certified as nursing facility eligible, able to live safely in the community with PACE support, dually eligible for Medicare/Medicaid.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage of all Medicare/Medicaid services plus extras like transportation, meals, and therapies; one monthly premium for Medicare-only.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays for low-income seniors. QMB covers full Part B costs, SLMB pays Part B premium, and QI offers sliding-scale premiums with extra Part D help.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+ (or disabled) meeting Hawaii-specific income/asset limits (e.g., QMB ~$1,255 individual income/month in 2026).
programs.benefit_value: QMB: Full Part B ($174.70/mo) + deductibles (~$240); SLMB: Part B premium; QI: Part B + Part D Extra Help up to $5,300 annual value.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly EBT benefits to buy healthy food at approved stores. In Hawaii, it supplements diets for low-income households including seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ or aged/blind/disabled households meeting income limits (e.g., ~$2,000/month for 1-person household), Hawaii residents.
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per person for groceries; max ~$500 for elderly/disabled single person.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: LIHEAP provides one-time grants to pay utility bills and connect/disconnect fees. It helps low-income households, including seniors, manage energy costs.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Hawaii households (seniors prioritized) at or below 60-150% federal poverty level depending on crisis status.
programs.benefit_value: Grants $200-1,000 per household annually for energy bills; crisis aid up to $400.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: WAP funds energy efficiency upgrades like insulation, sealing, and efficient appliances for low-income homes. It reduces energy costs and improves health/safety for seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households (seniors/disabled prioritized) at or below 200% FPL; administered via Honolulu Community Action Program (HCAP).
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements worth $5,000-10,000; average 20-30% utility savings.

programs.name: Sage PLUS (SHIP - State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Sage PLUS offers free, unbiased counseling on Medicare, Medigap, Part D, and low-income aids. Volunteers help seniors select plans and appeal denials.
programs.who_its_for: All Hawaii Medicare beneficiaries 65+, disabled, or pre-retirees seeking insurance guidance.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; saves thousands via optimal plan selection/appeals.

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals (Meals on Wheels)
programs.what_it_does: Provides nutritious meals delivered to homebound seniors' doors, often 5 days/week. Includes safety checks and nutrition counseling.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to shop/prepare meals, low-income prioritized via Area Agencies on Aging.
programs.benefit_value: ~250-300 meals/year at $5-10/meal (suggested donation); full federal funding for poorest.

programs.name: Executive Office on Aging Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Offers respite care, counseling, training, and supplies to relieve family caregivers. Helps sustain home care for seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid family caregivers of adults 60+ or grandparents raising children, income-eligible.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $500/month; training worth $200+; counseling sessions.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time paid training jobs in community nonprofits to unemployed low-income seniors. Includes job placement support.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income adults 55+ (income ≤125% FPL), priority to hardest-to-serve.
programs.benefit_value: 20 hrs/week at minimum wage (~$10.10/hr HI); workforce skills training.

programs.name: Legal Aid Society of Hawaii Elder Law Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal help for income-eligible seniors on benefits, housing, abuse, and estate planning. Includes representation and advice.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing legal issues with benefits, long-term care, or rights.
programs.benefit_value: Free attorney services worth $200+/hr; resolves benefit denials/housing disputes.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program (LTCOP)
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes/assisted living, resolving complaints on care, rights, and quality. Provides confidential support and education.
programs.who_its_for: Residents/families in HI long-term care facilities; open to all seniors.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving 80%+ complaints; protects against abuse/neglect.

programs.name: Kūpuna Aging in Place (KAP)
programs.what_it_does: The Hawaii Community Foundation provides multi-year grants to nonprofit organizations offering supportive services like community-based support, adult day care, day health, home-delivered meals, transportation, and caregiver support. This enables low- to moderate-income kūpuna (seniors) aged 65+ and their caregivers to age in place within their communities.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low- to moderate-income seniors aged 65 and older and their caregivers.[1]
programs.whats_unique: Hawaii-specific grant program from the Hawaii Community Foundation funding local nonprofits for culturally tailored aging-in-place services, including Native Hawaiian-focused support not replicated as a statewide foundation initiative elsewhere.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Over $3.7 million awarded in 2023 to 16 nonprofits for 3-year services; current 2026-2029 grants open.[1]

programs.name: Kūpuna Care Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides community-based long-term care services such as adult day care, attendant care, caregiver respite, case management, home-delivered meals, personal care, transportation, and homemaker services. Aims to help older adults with functional impairments remain at home as long as possible.[4]
programs.who_its_for: U.S. citizens or qualified aliens aged 60+ with functional impairments, not covered by comparable services and not residing in institutions.[4]
programs.whats_unique: County-funded (e.g., Honolulu Elderly Affairs Division) program integrating state and local resources for comprehensive home-based care tailored to Hawaii's aging population and high living costs.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Varied services including meals, transportation, and personal care; funded by federal, state, and county sources with no fixed dollar amount specified.[4]

programs.name: Senior Food Box Program (CSFP)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers monthly boxes of shelf-stable, nutrient-rich groceries to combat food insecurity among kūpuna. Supports health by reducing choices between food and other necessities, available on Oʻahu and Kauaʻi.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 60+ with household income at or below 150% of Hawaii Federal Poverty Guidelines.[6]
programs.whats_unique: Hawaii implementation of CSFP since 2015 through Hawaiʻi Foodbank, addressing island-specific food insecurity challenges with culturally sensitive distribution.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly food boxes; value equivalent to essential groceries, no fixed dollar amount listed.[6]
